design: design:core — MINIMAL SEMANTIC CORE (cells/slots + sealed invariants with tag erasure, confined views, machine-op/SIMD layer, scoped threads + closed atomics + custody, trusted boundary records; all blessed forms as checked libraries with zero trusted container internals)
lens: PERFORMANCE HOLES — common workloads where no blessed writing plausibly reaches the named SOTA par; mechanism-level (indirections, missed SIMD/inlining, forced copies/zeroing, extra branches/atomics, cache-line traffic); plus performance predictability and the optimizer-facts story

findings.title: The shared read-mostly hole is far wider than the declared reclamation GAP: there is no shared-immutable-ownership story at all
findings.detail: The design scopes its GAP to 'epoch/hazard reclamation' and routes read-mostly tables to (a) sharded mutex<map> or (b) snapshot-rebuild. Both routes fail mechanically. (a) A sharded-lock read still executes two RMWs on the shard lock word per lookup; on skewed key distributions (Zipf-distributed route/session/feature-flag lookups — the normal case, not the adversarial one) the hot shard's lock line ping-pongs across cores at ~40-100 ns per bounce, capping the whole table at roughly single-core throughput while papaya/evmap/folly wait-free reads (~15 ns, zero shared-line writes) scale linearly — a 10-20x gap at 16-32 cores. (b) The snapshot-rebuild workaround is arc-swap: readers hold a refcounted immutable table while a writer publishes a replacement. xlang has NO mechanism for this: single-owner affine values, no Arc/shared ownership, no 'static, lexical borrows only, and custody forbids reading slots not granted to you (which also structurally rules out seqlock's speculative-read-then-validate). Scoped borrows force a stop-the-world barrier at every table swap. Consequence: mundane, high-weight workloads — proxy/router config hot-reload, server session tables at high read fan-out, metrics snapshots, and notably a SHARED INTERNER in a parallel compiler (this project's own core audience; rustc parallelizes exactly this) — have zero blessed writing at par. This single missing capability silently degrades four coverage rows (concurrent map, concurrent keyed state, reclamation, the rwlock caveat), not one. Falsify: 32-core bench, 1M-entry table, 99.9% reads with Zipf(1.0) keys plus 1 reload/s; sharded mutex<map> and per-thread-merge vs arc-swap+papaya baselines; the design's forms should plateau below 4-core scaling.

findings.title: Every hot-loop PAR claim silently assumes cross-boundary inlining, in-place move ABI, and a bulk-op surface that the design never contracts
findings.detail: The design ships codegen CONTRACTS for exactly two things (1-load endian access, strict-select branchlessness) but the flagship numbers need three more it never states. (1) Inlining: vec push at ~1 ns amortized is 2-3 instructions ONLY when the sealed method body inlines into the user's loop; a non-inlined cross-module call is 5-10+ cycles of call/ret/register shuffle per element — a 3-5x cliff on push-, probe-, and masked-ring-op loops. Rust gets this via #[inline] + monomorphization in-crate; xlang forms are a separate project-authored library and the spec says nothing about mandatory inline-into-caller for sealed methods. (2) Move ABI: the affine, no-reborrow world pushes toward state-threading (take value, return value). For vec<T, INLINE=64> (a 512B+ object) or any threaded iterator/env struct, move-in/move-out without a guaranteed callee-in-place convention is a memcpy per call — the known SmallVector-by-value hazard, here made the default calling style. (3) Bulk ops: the vec contract lists push/pop/reserve/truncate but no extend_from_slice/append/drain/retain; building 1M elements by per-element push (predicted branch + store each) versus one reserve + move_range memcpy is 5-10x, and Rust reaches memcpy via specialization the catalog must instead ship explicitly. Falsify: extend experiment 1's asm gate with (a) form methods compiled in a separate unit, (b) a bulk-build benchmark (1M u64 into vec) with a memcpy-bandwidth pass bar, (c) asm of a threaded-iterator loop showing zero redundant struct copies.

findings.title: No specified iteration protocol: affine external iterators under ANF/no-closures plausibly miss hashbrown/slotmap iteration par
findings.detail: Coverage claims 'iteration in arbitrary order' for map, 'live-entry iteration near vec speed' for slab, and 'ordered iteration' for ordmap, but the design names no mechanism. Options in this kernel: (a) external affine iterator threaded through the loop — next(move it) -> (it, Option<&T>) — which adds an Option branch + state round-trip per element and, unless finding-2's inlining and move-ABI contracts hold, defeats the vectorized ctrl-bitmask walk hashbrown uses (~2-4 ns/element via movemask + x&=x-1 over 16-slot groups); (b) internal iteration via env-struct callback (for_each style), which restores the fused loop but requires guaranteed monomorphized callback inlining and is currently only an idiom, not a contract; (c) &uniq iteration (mutate-all-values, retain) has NO stated shape at all given no reborrowing — split_uniq exists for slices only, not for map/slab storage. Slab iteration must also skip vacant slots; 'near vec speed' requires the gens-parity scan to compile to a branchless or bitmask skip, which the sealed invariant does not automatically provide. Falsify: sum-over-1M-map-values and slab live-iteration benchmarks vs hashbrown 0.15 iter and slotmap 1.0 iter, with asm inspection for per-element call/branch overhead; pass bar within 1.2x.

findings.title: One-load varint/bit-refill decode collapses on borrowed inputs: the slack fact exists only for catalog-allocated buffers
findings.detail: The cursor kit claims upb-class decode: 'varint = one u64 load + mask, no byte loop' with a per-message requires fact. That unconditional 8B load at offset p is only safe when p+8 <= allocation end — upb guarantees it by REQUIRING 16 bytes of input slop. Where does xlang's slop fact come from? For iobuf-allocated network buffers the form can over-allocate and zero a 16B tail (cheap, fine — but note the tail must be LIVE-initialized, since reading vacant slots is exactly the forbidden uninit read). But two ubiquitous input classes have no slop: (a) mmap_ro file bytes (parquet/proto/capnp/ML-weight parsing straight off the map — a headline use of mmap_ro) and (b) subslices of larger buffers (zero-copy views of one frame inside a batch recv buffer), where the slice view erases the parent's slack unless the closed predicate/requires language can express and propagate 'k bytes readable past end within parent' through as_slice/subslice — a fact form the digest does not include. The fallback is per-field checked refill: a data-dependent branch and up to a byte loop per varint, ~2-3x per field on varint-dense protobuf, dropping the claimed 1-3 GB/s to upb-minus-half. Falsify: decode a protobuf corpus from (1) iobuf with slop, (2) mmap_ro, (3) mid-buffer subslices, vs upb/prost on the same host; PAR must hold on all three, not just (1).

findings.title: Wrong-form cliffs are invisible to the checker and the promised diagnostics contradict the frontend-simple ruling
findings.detail: The catalog contains several correct-but-slow traps with large multipliers: mpmc where spsc endpoints are actually unique (3-5x per op), sharded map for a read-hot table (10x+ at scale, per finding 1 — and here there is no fast sibling to point to), hint-strength select silently lowering to a branch in a user-written partition/clamp loop (5-10x on random data, detectable only by asm), per-element push where bulk move_range applies (5-10x), and INLINE=0 defaults on compiler-shaped millions-of-tiny-collections workloads (3-5x, the LLVM SmallVector lesson). The design's own mitigation — 'pool/channel diagnostics should name the faster sibling when endpoint linearity is provable' — requires whole-program endpoint-uniqueness analysis, which the settled frontend-simple/no-inference ruling forbids; so in practice the mitigation is a one-page decision table, whose efficacy for mid-tier writers is asserted, not evidenced (D9a tested sequential byte kernels, not concurrency form selection). Performance predictability is a stated owner goal; this design currently delivers safety predictability but not performance predictability at exactly the highest-multiplier choice points. Falsify: W1-style trial — 20 concurrency/collection tasks given to a mid-tier model with the decision table in context; measure wrong-form rate and realized slowdown; >20% wrong-form with >3x average penalty confirms.

findings.title: The wild-read tax on compression is understated and the frontier-read restriction also taxes SIMD tails
findings.detail: The design books the LZ4 decode gap at 10-20% citing lz4_flex-safe evidence, but published lz4_flex safe-vs-unsafe deltas and safe-Rust-vs-C LZ4 comparisons have historically run 25-40% on decompression (the wild READ across the initialized frontier is worth more than the wild write, because it eliminates the per-copy length/overlap branch chain in the match-copy loop, and match copies dominate decode). LZ4 decode sits on storage hot paths (RocksDB block reads, Parquet pages, zstd's LZ section) where 30% is a real regression against the 4-5 GB/s C baseline. Secondary effect the coverage does not book anywhere: the no-read-past-live rule forces every 64B block-classifier kernel (simdjson stage-1, simdutf) to handle final partial blocks by tail copy into a padded scratch or a masked/scalar epilogue — a fixed ~20-60 cycle cost per buffer that is negligible at 1MB but 10-30% on the many-small-inputs regime (validating thousands of short strings/headers), where simdutf uses overlapped full-width loads within bounds only when len>=width and page-trick overreads otherwise. Falsify: port the LZ4 match-copy loop under the margin-writer rules and measure vs liblz4 1.9 on Silesia (expect worse than the booked 0.8-0.9x), plus a 64-256B-input UTF-8 validation sweep vs simdutf.

findings.title: Growth path has no in-place extension: every growth is alloc+move_range, forfeiting realloc/mremap wins on large buffers
findings.detail: The design fixes growth as 'move_range at memcpy bandwidth' into a fresh allocation. Rust Vec and C++ vector on mimalloc/jemalloc grow via Allocator::grow/realloc, which extends in place when the successor block is free and, for multi-MB buffers, via mremap-style page remapping — turning an O(len) copy into O(1) page-table work. A 512MB append-heavy buffer (log ingestion, columnar builders, simdjson tape on big documents) pays ~15-20 ms of copy per doubling at 25-35 GB/s and double peak RSS, where mimalloc grows it in microseconds; growth-dominated phases can lose 1.5-2x end-to-end and OOM earlier at equal workload. The fix is one more TCB allocator record (try_grow_in_place) plus a move_range fallback — cheap, but it must be in the syscall/allocator record set from the start or the vec/bytebuf/tape PAR claims quietly exclude the large-buffer regime. Falsify: append 1GB of u64 one push at a time, xlang vec vs Rust Vec on mimalloc, measuring wall time and peak RSS; parity fails without the grow record.

overall: The stance is rescuable and its sequential core is genuinely strong — cells + sealed invariants + tag erasure is a credible route to Vec/SwissTable/VecDeque/slab machine-event parity, and the preregistered experiments would settle it — but the design as written over-claims PAR by leaning on unstated codegen guarantees (inlining, in-place move ABI, bulk ops, iteration protocol) and under-declares its concurrency hole: the missing capability is not "epoch reclamation" narrowly but shared-immutable ownership with deferred reclamation generally, without which arc-swap snapshots, read-scaling tables, seqlocks, and a shared parallel-compiler interner all have zero blessed writing at par while sharded locks plateau 10-20x below SOTA on skewed read-hot workloads. The single change that matters most: admit one built-in-TCB shared-snapshot/epoch-guard primitive (arc-swap/crossbeam-epoch-class) into v1 now — the stance's own "admission is evidence for a built-in" rule already points there — and simultaneously promote inlining and callee-in-place move to the same CI-gated codegen-contract class as the endian-load and strict-select promises, so the erasure experiments actually measure what the coverage table claims.

design: design:core — MINIMAL SEMANTIC CORE (cells/sealed invariants with tag erasure, confined views, machine-op/SIMD layer, scoped threads + custody atomics, trusted boundary records; all blessed forms as checked libraries with zero trusted container internals)
lens: SAFETY & TCB — forgeable authority at API boundaries, aliasing/reentrancy, drop/trap paths, concurrent publication/reclamation, honesty and verifiability of the TCB inventory, decidability of the new checker judgments

findings.title: mmap_ro lets externally mutable bytes forge validate-once facts: elided bounds checks over content another process can rewrite
findings.detail: Scenario: zero-copy typed views / checked cursor over mmap_ro (SQLite page walks, LMDB-style indexes). Mechanism: the design's whole F5 speed story is 'validate once, then every access is a raw load with the bounds check requires-elided'. But MAP_PRIVATE of a file does NOT freeze content — pages not yet CoW'd reflect external writes, silently, with no SIGBUS. So: validate a cell-pointer array or length field, external process rewrites that field, subsequent 'validated' accesses use an offset the checker proved in-bounds against bytes that no longer exist; if the elided check would have caught it, the load walks past the slice (and past the mapping edge → adjacent-mapping read or SIGBUS mid-vectorized-loop). This is exactly a forged fact channel: checker proofs double as optimizer facts (per the constitution), and here the proof's premises are writable by an unprivileged external process. The 'agreed-immutable file' contract is not enforceable (advisory locks don't bind other processes), and the promised 'defined deterministic SIGBUS trap' is neither deterministic (depends on external timing and which hoisted/reordered/vector load faults first) nor sufficient (content mutation raises no fault at all). Evidence to settle: two-process test — process A builds a typed view over mmap_ro, validates a data-dependent offset, spins; process B rewrites the offset field; observe whether A performs an access the elided check would have rejected. Fix direction: mmap-backed slices must be a distinct 'external bytes' type over which requires-facts derived from CONTENT (as opposed to the kernel-stable mapping length) are never cacheable/elidable — which costs the PAR claim for data-dependent-offset formats on mmap, and that downgrade should be stated.

findings.title: Role/writer tokens have no specified instance binding — type-level tokens are forgeable across instances, yielding a checked-source data race
findings.detail: Scenario: spsc ring (flagship custody derivation), and every custody form (mpmc, mutex). Mechanism: custody clauses name token TYPES (ProducerTok, ConsumerTok, Holder). Create two spsc rings A and B in one scope; hand B's consumer + A's producer token pair to one thread and the complementary mismatched pair to another. If tokens are ordinary affine values of a per-TYPE token type, the checker accepts push(&B, tokA) — now A's producer token authorizes single-writer plain-store lowering on B's tail while B's own producer also stores it: two writers on a 'single-writer' atomic with non-atomic-strength lowering = data race, T1 broken, entirely in 'checked' source. The standard fixes are all problematic here: Rust-style generativity uses invariant closure lifetimes (xlang has no closures); lexical-region branding requires one fresh region per ring instance, but rings are heap values moved across threads and tokens must be owned sendable values, not borrows — the digest's 'no borrows in data' and lexical-regions-only rules fight instance branding directly; a dynamic instance-id compare in the token adds a check to the 8-15 ns hot path and must itself be non-elidable. The design text never addresses this. Evidence to settle: write the token-binding rule; then the adversarial two-ring token-swap program must be rejected by the checker (or trapped at runtime) — if neither, the custody system is unsound as specified.

findings.title: Custody clause stability (interference freedom) is unchecked and no memory model is specified — concurrent forms are trusted internals with extra steps, falsifying the zero-trusted-internals headline
findings.detail: Scenario: all sealed concurrent forms. Mechanism: the judgment 'an observed metadata fact grants that range' is sound only if the fact is STABLE under every action other tokens are permitted — a rely-guarantee argument. Each shipped form leans on a different meta-argument the syntactic rule cannot see: spsc's cached-opposing-index requires stale-read-of-monotonic-counter ⇒ granted-range-is-a-subset (temporal monotonicity + cyclic subset reasoning); mutex requires the lock bit to be unwritable by others while held; Vyukov requires fetch_add result uniqueness plus seq epoch (pos vs pos+cap) reasoning the design itself flags as at-risk. Either the checker hardcodes exactly these three protocols (then the forms are compiler-known built-ins in disguise and the ~13 custody rules bought nothing general), or it accepts declared custody clauses on faith (then a wrong clause in any future project-authored form is a checker-accepted race, i.e., the clause is a trusted assertion — TCB, just not listed in the TCB inventory). Compounding: the kernel today has NO memory model; the acq/rel/publication axioms the syntactic ANF-order rule appeals to must be formalized somewhere and a soundness theorem ('custody-checked types are race-free under that model') proven — research-grade work absent from the ~50-rule budget and from the experiments (a loom pass on capacity-2 rings validates one implementation, not the judgment). Evidence to settle: state the stability side-condition precisely; demonstrate it accepts spsc/mpmc/mutex AND rejects seeded broken variants (relaxed load where acquire is needed; a grant clause over a non-monotonic field; a two-writer clause). If no such condition exists in the decidable fragment, re-badge the concurrent block honestly as builtin-TCB — which the stance's own fallback anticipates.

findings.title: Slab freelist invariant is outside the frozen predicate language; unconstrained scratch_read can forge free_head and overwrite a Live slot
findings.detail: Scenario: slab<T>, the keystone substrate (trees, graphs, intrusive links, timer wheel, the io-loop buffer registry all ride on it). Mechanism: the stated invariant 'vacant slots form a scratch-linked list rooted at free_head' is inductive reachability (acyclicity + coverage + closure), not expressible in the declared closed language (linear arithmetic, contiguous/cyclic range liveness, metadata biconditionals). So either the frozen language grows a reachability atom — the exact scope-creep-to-proof-search slide the design's own risk list forbids, and qualitatively worse than a new arithmetic atom — or the invariant is weakened to the gens-parity biconditional, in which case insert's pop of free_head consumes a scratch_read u64 the invariant does not constrain: a garbage or stale link makes insert perform write(Vacant→Live) on an index that is actually Live (erasure removed the tag that would catch it) → the old value's drop is skipped and two handles alias one slot. The rescue (dynamic re-check on pop: free_head < cap AND gens[free_head] has vacant parity, trap otherwise) is sound but adds a dependent load + branch to the 2-5 ns insert path and must be acknowledged in the PAR claim. Telling omission: the three preregistered experiments cover vec, SwissTable, ring, spsc — the slab, despite weight-high keystone status, is not among them. Evidence to settle: attempt the slab derivation in the prototype checker under the frozen language; record whether it needs a new atom, a dynamic check, or fails.

findings.title: Trap semantics vs. token quiescence and synthesized drop are unspecified: a trapped thread wedges or corrupts shared structure teardown
findings.detail: Scenario: any sealed concurrent form under 'trap propagation at join is defined'. Mechanism: the kernel doctrine is traps-to-the-boundary (deterministic abort), but scoped spawn promises trap PROPAGATION at join — implying a thread trap does not kill the process. Then: a producer that traps between claiming a Vyukov slot (fetch_add granted seq) and the publishing release store holds a grant no one can ever reclaim; a mutex holder that traps never clears the lock bit; and in all cases the trapped thread's role token is unreturnable, while join/drop of the sealed concurrent struct REQUIRES token quiescence — so the owning scope can neither drop the structure nor exit. The design must pick and specify one of: thread trap = whole-process abort (then 'propagation' is vacuous and leaks-on-abort are fine); or a poisoning discipline (token death mark, poisoned drop path) — which is new spec surface and new invariant states the closed language must cover; or scope-exit deadlock (unacceptable). Related sequential hazard: sealed invariants are checked at method EXIT; if any recovery/unwind path ever runs compiler-synthesized drop against a mid-method state (invariant temporarily broken during growth/split), erasure-synthesized drop double-frees or drops vacant slots as live. Evidence to settle: write the trap/join/drop semantics matrix; adversarial program with a producer trapping between slot write and publish, then scope exit — checker or runtime must have a defined, safe answer.

findings.title: Completion-loop drop with in-flight kernel ops: affine buffer moves do not model kernel DMA lifetime; dropping the loop frees memory the kernel will still write
findings.detail: Scenario: socket + completion event loop (io_uring backend). Mechanism: buffers move into the ring at submit and move back at completion — but the buffers' owned storage lives in a slab owned by the loop, and the ring SQ/CQ pages are mmaps owned by the loop. Drop the loop (early return, error path) with a slow multishot recv in flight: io_uring_queue_exit does not synchronously cancel in-flight ops; freeing the slab and unmapping rings while the kernel completes a DMA write is a kernel-side write into freed/reused heap — memory corruption outside the language's proof but entirely real, reachable from safe code via an ordinary drop. The ≤5-line-per-record TCB contract format cannot express the required obligation ('no registered buffer or ring page is freed until per-op cancellation completions have been reaped'); this is a structure-level quiescence protocol, exactly the class the design routed OUT of the decidable core for epoch reclamation. So the io loop needs either a blocking drop (drop that performs cancel+reap syscall loops — drops with effects, new spec territory), an abort-on-nonquiescent-drop rule, or leak-the-slab semantics — none is currently specified. Evidence to settle: write the loop's drop semantics; runtime test submitting a never-completing recv, dropping the loop, and watching (ASAN/strace) whether completion writes land in freed memory.

findings.title: The checker story omits loop invariants: per-method symbolic execution cannot verify data-dependent loops that transition slot states point-wise
findings.detail: Scenario: vec::truncate/drain (drop [new_len,len) one take at a time), map drop/clear sweeps, radix-sort scatter into cells scratch, B-tree bulk paths not expressible as one move_range. Mechanism: the design claims 'symbolic execution over ANF, no proof search, no fixpoints', but a loop with a data-dependent trip count that transitions one slot per iteration has no finite symbolic execution; soundly checking it requires an annotated loop invariant in the closed language (e.g., 'forall i in [new_len, j): vacant', j the induction variable) plus an inductiveness entailment — decidable, but a genuine addition: loop-invariant annotations are absent from the language_deltas, unbudgeted in the ~50 rules, and their absence would force either bounded unrolling (impossible for dynamic n) or bulk-op proliferation (a drop_range/take_range primitive per pattern, growing the cells op vocabulary instead). The claim 'formulas bounded by method size' survives only once this is added explicitly. Evidence to settle: run experiment 2's own 'vec grow/spill' plus a truncate derivation in the prototype checker; it will either demand loop annotations (then spec-cost them honestly) or reject every real method containing a state-transitioning loop.

findings.title: Arithmetic soundness gaps: ℤ-linear entailments vs wrapping machine integers, and u32 generation wrap silently forges stale-handle detection
findings.detail: Scenario: all sealed invariants (fields are u64/u32 with wrapping machine semantics) and slab's stale-handle contract. Mechanism: (a) the decidable fragment is quantifier-free LINEAR INTEGER arithmetic — if entailments are discharged over unbounded ℤ while code runs modulo 2^64, an invariant provable in ℤ can be false at wrap (e.g., head+len overflow in the cyclic clause, or the custody monotonicity fact 'counter never decreases'); the checker must either verify absence of overflow per operation (extra obligations, extra rejections) or reason in bitvector/modular arithmetic (different decision procedure than stated). u64 counters are practically unwrappable, but soundness claims cannot rest on 'practically'. (b) slab generations are u32: after 2^32 remove/insert cycles on one slot, a retained stale Copy handle's generation compares equal and get() hands out a different live object while the catalog card promises 'stale handles detected' — same accepted weakness as slotmap, but here it is an advertised checked contract, so it must be documented as probabilistic/bounded or the generation widened. Evidence to settle: (a) spec the checker's integer semantics and show one wrap-sensitive invariant (cyclic ring at head near 2^64) is handled; (b) arithmetic: 2^32 recycle loop test demonstrating the false-positive get.

overall: The stance is rescuable, and its sequential core is genuinely strong: under the kernel's own rules (no reborrowing, no borrows in heap data, affine moves, exhaustive match) the classic single-threaded attacks mostly fail — hasher/comparator reentrancy into a mid-operation container is structurally blocked because an env struct cannot capture the container while a unique borrow is live, entry/iterator aliasing appears typeable with lexical regions, and erasure risk is at least named and gated by experiments. The design's weak flank is everything whose safety argument is temporal or external rather than per-method-local: custody stability, token instance binding, trap/quiescence, io-loop kernel loans, and mmap-backed bytes — five of the eight findings are the same disease, a per-method syntactic checker asked to certify claims whose truth depends on other actors (other threads, the kernel, other processes) over time. The single most important change: split the claim honestly along that line — keep 'zero trusted internals' for the sequential cells/sealed/erasure catalog (where it is defensible and where most of the performance lives), and re-badge the concurrent custody block as what it currently is, a small set of expert-derived forms whose race-freedom rests on unproven meta-arguments — i.e., builtin-TCB with hostile review — unless and until a precise, decidable interference-freedom side-condition plus a stated memory model and soundness theorem are produced. Independently, mmap_ro must quarantine content-derived requires-facts (no elision from bytes an external process can rewrite) or the fact channel is forgeable and T1 falls; that one is not a re-badging question but a hard hole to close before any coverage claim for F5-on-mmap stands.

design: design:core — MINIMAL SEMANTIC CORE: cells/slots slot-state storage + sealed representation invariants with tag erasure + confined views + machine-op/SIMD layer + scoped threads/atomics/custody + trusted boundary records; all blessed forms as checked libraries with zero trusted container internals (~50 claimed new rules on the 90-rule v0.6 kernel).
lens: SPEC SIZE, TEACHABILITY, EVOLUTION — honest rule recount, mid-tier-writer form selection and API choreography, extensibility cliff, fit with the ANF/match-only/no-reborrow kernel, and whether this is n forms or n special cases in a uniform.

findings.title: Rule recount: the 50-rule estimate is a ~1.6-2.5x undercount, and the design fails its own preregistered >30% overrun bound on paper
findings.detail: Calibrate against what v0.6 actually charges per feature: buffer<T> — one trivial flat storage type with two ops — cost touches to TYPE-2, STOR-1, STOR-3, GRAM-3, two OP-1 row groups, and a dedicated edge rule OP-9 (~6 rule-units). FN-8 (one requires block) cost a grammar production, a full rule, an EFF-2 rewrite, and a reserved-word clause. Recosting each language_delta at that granularity: cells/slots = 8 ops each needing OP-1 rows + effects + OP-8-class edge semantics, novel bitmap-driven drop (STOR-3 rewrite), in-struct slots layout, sealed-only scoping, and the take/replace semantics STOR-1 explicitly says collections are blocked on → 12-18 rules; sealed types + the closed predicate language (atom syntax, semantics per atom, entailment fragment with pow2 axioms, abstraction boundary) → 8-12; erasure judgment + synthesized drop → 3-4; ensures/FN-8 extension → 3-5; confined view structs (structs cannot currently carry regions at all — new grammar, affinity, confinement, call-consumption, OWN-4/10 interaction) → 4-6; copy struct → 1-2; scalar machine ops + two codegen CONTRACTS (a new normative class: per-site verified codegen, beyond OP-8's stated lowerings) → 8-12; endian load/store → 2-3; SIMD value types (~8 new types, ~25 op families with lane/shuffle/movemask edge semantics, ISA-dispatch lifecycle, named-intrinsic escape) → 12-20; spawn/join + CAP-1 stub made real (Shareable/Sendable derivation per type) → 5-8; atomic vocabulary + publication semantics (the kernel has NO memory model today; even the 'syntactic publication rule' is a normative memory-model statement) → 6-8; single-writer refinement → 2-3; custody/tokens/quiescence → 8-13 (design's own number); trusted-record contract language + record class → 4-8; split_uniq → 1-2. Honest total: 79-124 rules, i.e. 88-138% growth on a 90-rule kernel. The design's own risk clause says a >30% overrun (>65 rules) falsifies the library-forms-cheaper premise — and the overrun is NOT concentrated in the severable custody block (cells+sealed+SIMD alone are ~35-54), so severing custody does not rescue it. Evidence to settle: draft the actual spec deltas for cells and SIMD alone at v0.6 granularity under META-3/META-5/META-6 discipline (every rule needs a derivation-ledger entry) and count; if those two land under 25 combined, this finding is wrong.

findings.title: The writer-facing catalog surface is uncounted shadow spec: 20 form cards x ~6-12 ops each is 150-250 normative signatures the D2 budget must absorb
findings.detail: The project's own no-shadow-spec principle (DIAG-3: 'writer-facing schemas above are counted spec mass') means the form cards are spec, not documentation. vec alone: with_capacity/push/pop/get/set/reserve/truncate/as_slice/as_uniq_slice/iterate/move-out, each with explicit region params, modes, effect rows, and trap-vs-Result classification per ERR-4 — ~12 signatures; times ~20 forms plus 8 composition recipes plus the form-selection decision table plus per-form failure semantics. Today's entire prelude is 8 declarations. Under TYPE-5 (all type/region/const args explicit at every call site) each signature must be learned exactly, so this surface sits inside the model's context window alongside the kernel — which is precisely the D2 scarce resource. The design implicitly assumes a two-tier spec (writer kernel vs sealed-implementer annex) to make '~50 rules, writers never see cells/sealed' work, but no partition mechanism exists in v0.6 (SCOPE-1 defines one document), and inventing one is itself a meta-rule change. Evidence to settle: write the vec + map + slab cards in full normative form and measure token count against the current 408-line spec; if the three cards exceed ~30% of the existing kernel's mass, the n-small claim is being achieved by moving mass off the books, not by elegance.

findings.title: Frozen invariant language pins the catalog to day-1 SOTA: the first algorithm-level upgrade of a form strands it, inverting the 'libraries evolve cheaper than built-ins' premise
findings.detail: The closed predicate language (linear arithmetic over fields, contiguous/cyclic-pow2 range liveness, metadata biconditionals) is fitted exactly to the day-1 forms — which is suspicious, and the design's own risk list admits B-tree split/merge and tombstone rebalancing already pressure it. Concrete evolution test: Robin Hood hashing with backward-shift deletion (a plausible SwissTable successor for some workloads, and F14's chunk-displacement metadata is similar) requires an invariant relating each slot's occupancy to a DATA-DEPENDENT quantity — probe_distance(i) = (i - h(key_at(i))) mod cap — which is a per-slot function of stored key content, categorically outside linear-arithmetic-over-fields. Same for any displacement, cuckoo, or ordered-probe scheme. So when hashbrown 0.17-era SOTA moves, the checked map cannot follow without a spec change to the frozen language (per the design's own escalation rule, the form then becomes a built-in — i.e., the stance's zero-trusted-internals core erodes on the first SOTA shift). A trusted built-in's internals, by contrast, track SOTA inside the TCB with zero spec delta. Evidence to settle: attempt to state the backward-shift Robin Hood invariant, or the actual hashbrown tombstone-rebalance-on-insert invariant, in the frozen atom set; if either needs a new atom, evolution cost is proven, not hypothetical.

findings.title: Entry API and iteration may be unteachable at the one-hash contract under no-reborrow + call-consumes-&uniq: mid-tier writers will silently degrade to two-hash patterns
findings.detail: The map form's par claim depends on entry-style get-or-insert (demand map: 'one hash, one probe — critical, counters and caches live on this'). In this kernel, entry(&uniq 'r m, k) must return a borrow-carrying confined view (an Occupied/Vacant enum with &uniq payloads — note: the design only specifies confined view STRUCTS, so borrow-carrying enums are an unstated extension), which is affine, consumed by any call, non-reborrowable, and must be dispatched via a GIVE-1 let-initializer match with explicit regions at every step. The D9a first-green evidence (percent-encoding, utf8parse) covers sequential byte loops — nothing like this choreography. The known LLM failure mode under borrow pressure is retreat to the pattern that typechecks: contains_key-then-insert or get-then-insert, which is correct-but-slow (2x hash+probe on the hottest map idiom) and invisible to the checker — exactly the hazard class the design itself flags for form selection, but here it is INSIDE a single form's usage, so no decision table catches it. Iteration has the same shape: no closures, no for, so every map/slab traversal is an iterator view + loop + per-iteration match on Option, with OWN-11 forcing fresh borrow regions inside the loop body. Evidence to settle: the W1 experiment the design never registers — give a mid-tier model the map card and the task 'word-frequency count'; measure first-green rate and the fraction of solutions using entry vs double-lookup. If <half use entry correctly, the par claim for the read-modify-write workload fails on teachability grounds even though the form itself is at par.

findings.title: Two aliasing systems with an unspecified interface: OWN-7's literal-only index disjointness cannot support sealed-code borrows or multi-slot mutation, and the fix multiplies form APIs
findings.detail: Outside sealed code, overlap is OWN-7: two index places conflict unless both indices are unequal LITERALS. Inside sealed code, the new checker reasons over symbolic ranges. But borrows cross the boundary: get(slab, h) -> &'r T roots in slot content and is judged by OWN-5/6/7. Consequence: two live &uniq borrows into different slots of one slab are unconditionally rejected (both resolve to the slab root, indices symbolic), yet the tree/intrusive-link PAR claims are priced as 'detach/reattach = 3-5 index stores' touching parent, child, AND sibling nodes simultaneously. In this kernel that is 3-5 separate get_mut round trips (each a bounds + generation check, no two borrows concurrently live), or take/put-back cycles, or the command-buffer pattern — plausibly 2-3x ego-tree/rowan unless every check is proof-elided across calls, which the frontend-simple checker explicitly does not do inter-procedurally. The standard fix (get2_uniq/get_disjoint_uniq ops per form, Rust's get_disjoint_mut) works but sprouts arity-N variants across slab/vec/map, inflating the finding-2 surface and violating one-spelling-per-meaning in spirit. The spec must also state which judgment owns a borrow that escapes a sealed method — currently unwritten, and it is exactly where erasure-adjacent miscompile bugs would live. Evidence to settle: write the tree detach/reattach method in the proposed kernel and count dynamic checks and round trips in the generated code vs the C pointer baseline; and try to state the sealed-to-OWN borrow handoff rule in one page.

findings.title: Extensibility cliff: any out-of-catalog structure with inline affine-element occupancy is inexpressible outside sealed, and the sealed pipeline is owner-gated — the fallback is a 1.2-3x tax plus organizational latency
findings.detail: cells/sealed is exactly the capability that makes flat partially-occupied storage writable, and it is exactly what ordinary writers are forbidden to touch. First realistic collisions: an ART/radix-tree index (Node4/16/48 with partially-filled key arrays holding AFFINE values), a rope/gap buffer for an editor, a priority structure with intrusive decrease-key, an LSM memtable skiplist. Copy-payload variants survive via the slab+u32-handle escape (genuinely good — credit where due), but affine-payload flat nodes must either box every element (the demand map's named fail shape, 3-5x on allocation-dominated workloads) or route payloads through a second slab and store handles (extra indirection + generation check per access, ~1.2-2x). The doctrinally correct path — 'catalog gap is a project finding' — means waiting on project-authored sealed code with hostile review, the same serialization point the design already flags for the ~30 TCB records; for a working team that is weeks of latency per gap, and the design ships with known day-1 gaps (reclamation, Chase-Lev, writable mmap) already queued ahead. Evidence to settle: implement an ART with owned string values using only catalog forms; if it lands >1.5x behind a C ART on the point-lookup workload, the cliff is a performance trap, not a graceful degradation — and the catalog-gap queue length after one real project (e.g., porting SQLite's pager+btree) measures the organizational cost.

findings.title: Doctrine collision: slab's checked recycling reverses the ratified P2 never-recycle rejection, leaving two blessed identity substrates with no selection rule
findings.detail: STOR-1 (v0.6) normatively records 'the arena-index-pool ownership pattern is rejected as a collection basis (it resurrects use-after-free as well-typed slot-recycling)', and P2 (never-recycled u64 indices) is a ratified blessed pattern. The slab form re-legalizes recycling under generation checks — defensible, but it is a re-decision, and the design presents it as an addition rather than a supersession. Teaching consequence: the catalog now contains BOTH the never-recycled append-only pool (P2, the current graph substrate) and the generational slab, plus arena — three identity/storage forms whose selection differences (mass free vs individual free vs never-free-growing) are performance- and correctness-relevant but checker-invisible; a mid-tier writer told 'P2 is the blessed graph substrate' by old doctrine and 'slab is the tree/graph substrate' by the new cards has two contradictory teachings and R3 (one spelling per meaning) has no answer. Also every slab get returns Option, so writers must write a None arm at millions of sites where staleness is logically impossible — expect either boilerplate trap arms or models inventing bogus recovery paths. Evidence to settle: write the one-page decision table entry distinguishing P2-pool/slab/arena and have a mid-tier model classify 10 scenarios; and record the P2 supersession as an explicit re-decision rather than shipping both.

findings.title: Trap propagation at join contradicts EFF-4 trap-is-abort; the concurrency block's kernel integration is under-specified beyond custody
findings.detail: EFF-4/SCOPE-4: a trap aborts the process with no unwinding and no cleanup. The threads form says 'join returns the thread's value; trap propagation at join is defined' — but if any thread's trap aborts the process, there is nothing to propagate at join; if instead a child trap is caught and surfaced at join, that IS unwinding-like cleanup semantics (who runs the child's region drops? what happens to a spsc ring's token quiescence when its holder aborted?), contradicting the no-unwinding rule and interacting with custody's join/drop-requires-token-quiescence rule (a trapped holder never returns its token, so every sibling join deadlocks or the rule needs a poisoned-token state — new rules again). Same class of unpaid bill: EFF-1 effect rows have no vocabulary for 'shared with another thread', and CAP-1's Shareable/Sendable need per-type derivation rules for every new type (cells, atomics, views, tokens). Small individually, but they show the ~13-rule custody estimate covers only the novel part of concurrency, not its integration with sections 5, 9, and 13 — reinforcing finding 1. Evidence to settle: write the join semantics rule and the trap-in-thread story in one page without touching EFF-4; if it needs a poisoned-token or partial-abort concept, the concurrency block grows again.

overall: The sequential core of the stance is genuinely good — cells+sealed+erasure is one mechanism that honestly unifies vec/ring/map/btree/slab/iobuf, and that part deserves to survive — but the stance as costed is not credible: an honest recount at v0.6's own granularity yields 79-124 rules against the claimed 50, blowing the design's own preregistered >30% overrun bound before any experiment runs, and the overrun lives mostly OUTSIDE the severable custody block, while a further 150-250 signatures of form cards sit uncounted despite the project's no-shadow-spec principle. The single change that matters most: re-cost honestly and re-partition — keep cells/sealed for the high-coverage-per-rule sequential container family, move the concurrency forms (spsc/mpmc/mutex, whose 13+ custody rules purchase only three forms and already exclude Chase-Lev and reclamation) and the long SIMD tail to built-in-TCB or a formally partitioned implementer annex, and make the two-tier spec (writer surface vs sealed-implementer surface) an explicit mechanism so D2 is measured on what a writer's context window actually loads. Without that repartition the design is n special cases wearing a uniform at roughly double the claimed spec price; with it, the strongest ideas survive at a price the kernel can pay — subject to the teachability experiments (entry-API choreography, multi-slot mutation) that the current experiment list omits entirely in favor of performance parity.
